content:
History Physical:
On August 5, 2024, the patient presented with fatigue, generalized weakness, and inability to exercise, accompanied by exertional dyspnea. Clinical signs of sarcopenia were present. He reported urinary incontinence following prior prostate cancer surgery (Gleason 7b). He had pneumonia three weeks earlier, which was treated with intravenous antibiotics at another hospital. Persistent transfusion-dependency (1/week).
Physical examination: Pale conjunctivae and reduced muscle mass consistent with sarcopenia. Mild exertional dyspnea observed on ambulation. No lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Cardiopulmonary examination otherwise unremarkable. Surgical scar in the pelvic region; mild urinary incontinence reported. No peripheral edema. Stable weight over the past month.
Current medication: Metformin 1,000 mg 1-0-1, empagliflozin 10 mg 1-0-0, ramipril 5 mg 1-0-0, amlodipine 5 mg 1-0-0, bisoprolol 5 mg 1-0-1, apixaban 2.5 mg 1-0-0, atorvastatin 20 mg 1-0-0.

content:

Na 136 mmol/L, K 3.3 mmol/L, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR 55 mL/min/1.73 m²), AST 75 U/L, ALT 73 U/L, bilirubin 1.4 mg/dL, LDH 325 U/L, uric acid 7.1 mg/dL.

content:
Ct Thorax Abdomen:
CT scan (August 6, 2024): Thoracic and abdominal contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated residual linear scarring in the right lower lobe compatible with prior pneumonia. A solitary pulmonary nodule (1.2 cm) with peripheral ground-glass halo in the right lower lobe was identified. No pleural effusion or mediastinal lymphadenopathy was seen. Mild splenomegaly and hepatic steatosis were confirmed; no focal hepatic or osseous lesions detected.

content:
Ultrasound Abdomen:
August 6, 2024: Abdominal ultrasound: Mild splenomegaly (14.5 cm) and hepatic steatosis with smooth liver margins and no focal lesions. The gallbladder appeared unremarkable, and intrahepatic bile ducts were not dilated. Postoperative changes consistent with prior prostate cancer surgery were noted in the pelvic region. The right kidney showed normal size and parenchymal structure, while the left kidney appeared shrunken (8.1 cm) with reduced cortical thickness and preserved perfusion. No ascites or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy detected.

solve.working_diagnosis: Severe pancytopenia with transfusion dependence most consistent with a bone-marrow failure syndrome, highly suspicious for myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) or MDS/AML overlap (await bone marrow morphology/cytogenetics in round 2)
differentials: Bone marrow infiltration (e.g., lymphoma, metastatic solid tumor including prostate cancer)
Hypersplenism/portal hypertension (less likely given only mild splenomegaly)
Drug/toxin-related marrow suppression or severe nutritional deficiency (B12/folate/copper; not yet assessed)
Hemophagocytic syndrome or severe infection-related marrow suppression (needs round-2 infectious workup)

treatment_plan.action: Hold anticoagulation (apixaban) given platelet count 18×10^9/L and reassess thromboembolic vs bleeding risk; institute bleeding precautions

Supportive care: transfuse packed RBCs as needed for symptoms/goal Hb ~8–9 g/dL; transfuse platelets for bleeding or prophylactically per local thresholds (e.g., <10×10^9/L, higher if febrile/procedure); consider G-CSF only if febrile neutropenia or recurrent infections after hematology review
Urgent hematology workup coordination for definitive diagnosis: bone marrow aspirate/biopsy with morphology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics/FISH, and myeloid NGS; include assessment for fibrosis

Infection risk mitigation: educate on neutropenic precautions; prompt evaluation for fever; given solitary pulmonary nodule with halo sign, arrange expedited infectious evaluation and repeat chest imaging/ID consult when available to rule out invasive fungal infection before any disease-directed therapy
Assess reversible contributors where feasible (not yet available in case set): B12/folate/copper, viral serologies, hemolysis labs; review medication/toxin exposures

If MDS confirmed: risk-stratify (IPSS-R/IPSS-M) and discuss disease-modifying therapy—hypomethylating agent (azacitidine/decitabine) for higher-risk disease vs ESA/luspatercept for lower-risk anemia; consider allogeneic transplant candidacy only if fit and high-risk; continue supportive transfusions and manage iron overload if long-term transfusion dependent

content:
Bone Marrow:
Bone marrow (July 28, 2024): Morphology revealed hypocellular bone marrow with 25–30% myeloblasts. Dysplastic changes were noted across myeloid lineages, characterized by toxic granulation and hypolobulated nuclei. Erythropoiesis and granulopoiesis were markedly reduced; megakaryocytes were decreased and dysplastic.

solve.working_diagnosis: Hypocellular acute myeloid leukemia (AML) evolving from an underlying myelodysplastic syndrome (25–30% marrow myeloblasts with multilineage dysplasia)
differentials: High-grade MDS with excess blasts (borderline blast count; classify as AML if ≥20% blasts per current criteria)
Therapy-related myeloid neoplasm (given prior prostate cancer treatment history; needs confirmation of chemo/radiation exposure and cytogenetics)
Aplastic anemia with clonal evolution to AML
Bone marrow–infiltrating malignancy with secondary dysplasia (less likely with 25–30% myeloblasts)

treatment_plan.action: Initiate AML inpatient supportive care: neutropenic precautions; empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately if febrile; transfuse RBCs/platelets per thresholds; stop apixaban given platelets 18×10^9/L unless compelling indication and after hematology/cardiology discussion

Complete urgent AML diagnostic classification/risk workup on marrow/blood: flow cytometry immunophenotype; conventional cytogenetics/FISH; rapid molecular testing (FLT3, NPM1, CEBPA, TP53, IDH1/2 and myeloid NGS) to guide prognosis and targeted therapy eligibility

Assess fitness and choose induction strategy: if fit consider intensive induction (e.g., 7+3 ± midostaurin if FLT3+); if unfit/older or significant comorbidity use lower-intensity regimen azacitidine or decitabine plus venetoclax; consider CPX-351 if therapy-related AML or AML with myelodysplasia-related changes and patient is intensive-eligible

Evaluate pulmonary nodule with halo sign in context of neutropenia before/while starting AML therapy: infectious diseases consult; obtain fungal diagnostics and consider pre-emptive/empiric mold-active antifungal (e.g., voriconazole/isavuconazole) if clinical suspicion is high
Early transplant planning if adverse-risk biology and patient is candidate: HLA typing and referral to transplant service once cytogenetics/molecular risk available

solve.working_diagnosis: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with myelodysplasia-related features, hypocellular, presenting with severe pancytopenia (25–30% marrow blasts) and adverse-risk cytogenetics (multiple structural abnormalities)
differentials: Therapy-related myeloid neoplasm (t-AML/t-MDS) if prior radiotherapy/chemotherapy exposure is confirmed
High-grade MDS/AML overlap (less likely given ≥20% blasts)
Aplastic anemia with clonal evolution to AML (hypocellular marrow)

treatment_plan.action: Stabilize and optimize supportive care: RBC and platelet transfusions as clinically indicated; strict bleeding precautions; discontinue/withhold apixaban at current platelet count unless an overriding indication after cardiology input; neutropenic precautions and prompt empiric IV antipseudomonal antibiotics for any fever

Finalize AML workup needed for definitive risk/therapy selection: rapid/complete myeloid molecular panel (FLT3, NPM1, IDH1/2, TP53, etc.), baseline infection assessment (blood cultures if febrile; evaluate pulmonary halo nodule for invasive fungal disease), and document prior chemo/radiation to classify as therapy-related if applicable

Start disease-directed therapy appropriate for age/comorbidity and adverse-risk biology: prefer lower-intensity azacitidine (or decitabine) plus venetoclax if not a good candidate for intensive induction; if deemed fit for intensive therapy and t-AML/AML-MR, consider CPX-351 per institutional standards

Manage suspected fungal lung lesion in neutropenic AML: obtain ID consult and initiate/continue mold-active antifungal therapy (e.g., voriconazole or isavuconazole) with drug–drug interaction planning if using venetoclax

Transplant strategy: if remission achieved and functional status permits, proceed with donor search/HLA typing and refer for allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation given adverse-risk cytogenetics; if not transplant-eligible, continue HMA±venetoclax with palliative/supportive focus and goals-of-care discussions
